findings.description: Bare yaml.load(f) without a SafeLoader is the textbook arbitrary-code-execution sink (e.g. !!python/object/apply:os.system) — use yaml.safe_load for untrusted config.
Precedence is inverted vs the documented 'env > file > defaults': env is applied before file, so dict.update lets the file silently override environment variables.
require() uses get() with fallback=None then rejects 'value is None', so a key legitimately set to null/None is indistinguishable from a missing key and wrongly raises.

get() calls load(), re-reading the file, re-parsing YAML, and rescanning os.environ on every single-key access; feature_enabled() and require() inherit this so each lookup repeats full disk+parse work.
Env overlay flattens APP_FEATURE_FLAGS to a coerced scalar string that clobbers the dict default, offers no way to set nested keys, and maps a bare 'APP_' prefix to the empty-string key.
_coerce aggressively guesses types with no opt-out, so legitimate string config like a version '1.0' becomes float 1.0 and 'true' becomes a bool, corrupting intended string values.
open() on config_path has no guard against symlink/path-traversal of an attacker-controlled config_path and no try/except around file read for IOError/PermissionError.
INFO log emits the config file path and key count on every load(); combined with the per-access reload this is noisy and mildly leaks deployment layout.
